sections.label: USER PROMPT
sections.content:
The code executed 2 actions in the previous sequence.
Executed actions: DOWN, RIGHT.
You are still on the same level.
Current state: step 48, level 1.
Valid actions right now: UP, DOWN, LEFT, RIGHT, MOUSE.
Only tool: `python`. It receives `current_frame`, `previous_frame`, `history`, `transitions`, `last_transition`, `valid_actions`, `last_action_result`, and `action(actions)`. It also provides `last_animation` (per-action frames of your last `action(...)` call) and `frame_stats` (how much this game has animated so far) -- optional; use them only if you judge the intermediate motion informative rather than incidental. REQUIRED: call `predict(expect_change=... )` (or `predict(expected_grid=...)`) before each `action(...)`; then read `last_prediction_result`/`prediction_stats`. A `surprise` marks where your world model is wrong -- re-probe there instead of pressing on.
Only letter-coded board views and lightweight metadata are exposed; raw numeric color IDs are not available.
Keep tool output compact: use `current_frame.segmentation` as the primary view, and `current_frame.ascii` only for a small specific region; never print full boards.
For the most recent change, compare `previous_frame` to `current_frame`, or `last_transition.before_frame` to `last_transition.after_frame`; `history[-1].frame` is the current frame, not the previous one.
Use Python to inspect the evidence, refine that world model from the newest history, and search or score candidate actions or short sequences against the current goal as you currently understand it.
Maintain a compact working world model of what the current level seems to contain, what actions appear to do, what the goal seems to be, what is still uncertain, and what plan currently looks best.
Below you are provided with the current world model from the previous turn. The default behavior is to copy it and add or remove things based on the evidence that you gathered. BEFORE EXECUTING NEW ACTIONS YOU MUST ALWAYS GIVE THE REVISED VERSION OF THE WORLD MODEL.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it, but stop immediately if a result reports `game_over`, `run_complete`, `level_completed`, or `done`.
Working world model carried from earlier turns:
- World model: The board is split vertically by a white column divider at col 32-33. Left side has a dark gray background with several colored objects. Right side has a black background with objects. Need to understand the object layout and goal.
- Revise any item above immediately if `current_frame` or `history` contradicts it.
end of world model. 
Focus on what changed most recently in `history`, update the target environment change if needed, and separate gameplay-object changes from HUD-only changes.
When ready, call `action(actions)` from inside the `python` tool with the best valid action or ordered batch selected by your code. If your code has found a reliable short sequence, prefer batching it in one call.
You may call `action(actions)` more than once in one Python snippet if your search or control loop needs it.
If you include assistant text before a tool call, keep it short and use it to update the world model. Helpful optional prefixes are `World model:`, `Goal model:`, `Action model:`, `Recent findings:`, `Open questions:`, `Plan:`, and `Cross-level notes:`.
When calling `python`, emit exactly the tool-call format shown elsewhere in this prompt for this model. Use only that format; do not add markdown fences, prose wrappers, or alternate tool-call syntax. Do not quote or place tool-call markup inside explanatory text; when you decide to call the tool, emit the tool call itself.
If you use MOUSE, include integer row and col arguments.
EFFICIENCY BUDGET — your score on each level is (baseline_actions / your_actions)^2, so every wasted action costs you quadratically.
Level 1: you have used 47 of about 59 target actions so far.

sections.label: THINKING
sections.content:
The light green square is now at [42:43, 12:13] (bottom-right of the bottom gray box). The level didn't complete.
Let me think about this more systematically. I've been trying many things:
1. Moving the light green square to different positions
2. Clicking on the right shapes to rearrange the left blocks
3. Trying to restore the initial configuration
None of these have completed the level. Let me think about what I haven't tried yet.
Maybe the game is about clicking on the left blocks, not the right shapes. Let me try clicking on the left blocks and see what happens.
Or maybe the game is about clicking on the colored squares. Let me try clicking on the light green square and see what happens.
Or maybe the game is about clicking on the gray boxes. Let me try clicking on the top gray box and see what happens.
Actually, let me step back and think about the game more carefully. The right shapes have a "stepped" pattern. The left blocks rearrange when I click on the right shapes. The colored squares move when I click on the right shapes or use arrow keys.
Maybe the game is about "painting" the left blocks to match the right shapes. The colored squares are "brushes" that paint the blocks. When I click on a right shape, it "paints" the corresponding left block with that shape's color.
But I haven't seen any evidence of the blocks being painted. The blocks just rearrange when I click on the right shapes.
Wait, let me look at the left blocks more carefully. The left blocks are:
- Blue rect: [20:23, 18:21] (4x4) - top-right
- Gray: [30:33, 8:11] (4x4) - middle-left
- Red: [30:33, 12:17] (4x6) - middle-center (horizontal)
- Dark red: [30:33, 18:21] (4x4) - middle-right
- Checkerboard: [34:37, 8:11] (4x4) - bottom-left
The blue rect is blue, which matches the blue shape's color. The red block is red, which matches the red shape's color. The gray block is gray, which doesn't match any shape's color. The dark red block is dark red, which doesn't match any shape's color. The checkerboard is checkerboard, which doesn't match any shape's color.
Maybe the game is about arranging the left blocks to match the right shapes' colors. The red shape is red, so the red block should be adjacent to the red shape's position on the left side. The blue shape is blue, so the blue block should be adjacent to the blue shape's position on the left side.
The red shape is at the top-right of the right panel, and the blue shape is at the bottom-right. The red block needs to be at the top-right of the left panel, and the blue block needs to be at the bottom-left.
But the current configuration has the blue block at the top-right and the red block in the middle. The red block is not at the top-right.
Wait, maybe the game is about arranging the left blocks to match the right shapes' positions. The red shape is at the top-right of the right panel, so the red block should be at the top-right of the left panel. The blue shape is at the bottom-right of the right panel, so the blue block should be at the bottom-right of the left panel.
The current configuration has the blue block at the top-right and the red block in the middle. The red block is not at the top-right, and the blue block is not at the bottom-right.
To match the right shapes' positions, I need:
- Red block at top-right of left panel
- Blue block at bottom-right of left panel
But the current configuration has the blue block at the top-right and the red block in the middle. So I need to swap the blue and red blocks.
But I don't know how to swap the blocks. Clicking on the right shapes rearranges the blocks, but I'm not sure about the exact rules.
Let me try clicking on the red shape and see what happens.

sections.label: TOOL CALL: python
sections.content:
<tool_call>
<function=python>
<parameter=code>
# Let me try a systematic approach to understand what clicking on right shapes does
# First, let me record the current state of left blocks

seg = current_frame.segmentation
blocks = {}
for n in seg['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'R', 'g', 'r', 'S']:  # blue, red, gray, dark red, sky blue (checkerboard)
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        blocks[n['color']] = (r0, c0, r1, c1)
        print(f"{n['color']}: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

print(f"\nCurrent: {blocks}")

# Let me try clicking on the red shape and see what changes
predict(expect_change=True, note='Click red shape - track block changes')
action([{'action': 'MOUSE', 'row': 18, 'col': 48}])

seg2 = current_frame.segmentation
blocks2 = {}
for n in seg2['nodes']:
    if n['color'] in ['b', 'R', 'g', 'r', 'S']:
        b = n['boundary']
        r0, r1 = min(p[0] for p in b), max(p[0] for p in b)
        c0, c1 = min(p[1] for p in b), max(p[1] for p in b)
        blocks2[n['color']] = (r0, c0, r1, c1)
        print(f"{n['color']}: [{r0}:{r1},{c0}:{c1}]")

print(f"\nAfter red click: {blocks2}")

# Compare
for color in blocks:
    if color in blocks2:
        if blocks[color] != blocks2[color]:
            print(f"\n{color} changed: {blocks[color]} -> {blocks2[color]}")
    else:
        print(f"\n{color} disappeared")

for color in blocks2:
    if color not in blocks:
        print(f"\n{color} appeared: {blocks2[color]}")
</parameter>
</function>
</tool_call>
